problem_summary: 2011 MCM/ICM Problem C (interdisciplinary): assess the wide-ranging economic, environmental, social, and health impacts of widely adopting electric cars as a means of transportation. Team #11325's solution reframes the open-ended policy question as a quantitative market-dynamics problem: it models the competition between three vehicle populations — gasoline ICE, plug-in hybrid (PHEV), and battery-electric (BEV) — using an adapted three-species Lotka-Volterra ecological competition model, then propagates the predicted market-share trajectories into downstream estimates of CO2 emissions, criteria-pollutant emissions, oil consumption, electricity demand, jobs, and health costs. A separate present-worth economic analysis compares a Nissan Leaf vs. a Honda Civic to judge BEV cost-competitiveness and the level of government subsidy needed.
models_used: Three-species Lotka-Volterra interspecific competition model (system of 3 coupled logistic ODEs for ICE G(t), BEV E(t), PHEV H(t)) with per-pair competition coefficients alpha, beta, gamma and shared carrying-capacity terms K
Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg (RKF45) adaptive-step ODE integrator with Cash-Karp coefficients (4th/5th-order embedded error estimate, step size adjusted to bound local error)

Present-worth / discounted-cash-flow economic model: PV = -C0 + L*SPPW(i,n) + sum_t (B_t - R_t)/(1+i)^t, with effective annual rate i_eff = (1+I/k)^k - 1, applied to an 8-year vehicle life cycle (Leaf vs. Civic)
Single Payment Compound Amount (SPCA = (1+i)^n) used to bring historical MSRP to present value for resale-value estimation
Scenario analysis: 5 parameter regimes (base, heavy EV investment, expensive electricity, high oil price, best case) realized by perturbing initial conditions and competition coefficients

Downstream linear emission-factor / consumption accounting models driving the population trajectories through per-mile factors from the GREET model (CO2, NOx, SOx, PM10, PM2.5, CH4, CO, VOCs), oil consumption, and added electricity demand
Local one-at-a-time sensitivity analysis of the economic model (salvage value L, maintenance Mc, $/kWh, kWh/km efficiency, $/gal, interest rate i)
key_techniques: Analogy transfer: mapping an ecology model (species competing for a limited food resource) onto an economic market (vehicle types competing for limited consumer dollars), with carrying capacity = total addressable vehicle fleet and competition coefficients = relative consumer-preference advantage

Encoding policy/economic levers as model parameters: gasoline price -> raises alpha (BEV's effect on ICE); electricity price -> raises ICE competitiveness vs. BEV; infrastructure investment -> 10x boost to initial BEV/PHEV populations; battery tech -> growth rate and efficiency
Carrying capacity tied to a real exogenous driver: K grows at 1%/yr matching US population growth (World Bank), grounding the model in demographic reality

Using a single scalar summary metric for cross-scenario comparison: the 'equivalence year' when ICE count G equals BEV count E (e.g., 2035 base, 2025 with investment, 2042 expensive electricity, 2028 high oil, 2018 best case)
Semi-log plotting because vehicle populations span many orders of magnitude (0.01M BEVs to 246M ICEs) over the horizon

Two-stage pipeline: market-share dynamics first, then multiply trajectories by per-mile emission/consumption factors (GREET, EPA, EIA) to convert abstract populations into the impact quantities the problem actually asks about
Grounding initial conditions in real registration data: G(0)=246M ICE, E(0)=0.01M BEV, H(0)=0.1M PHEV
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on three axes. (1) Interpretation of an open-ended interdisciplinary prompt into a single coherent, quantitative engine: rather than writing a qualitative essay on EV pros/cons, the team built a market-dynamics simulator whose outputs (market share over time) are directly converted into every dimension the problem asks about — economic, environmental, social, health. That structural unity is rare and impressive. (2) The central modeling choice is creative yet principled: adapting Lotka-Volterra competition to a three-product market is a genuine insight, not a stock model, and the team justifies the analogy term-by-term (resource = consumer dollars, K = fleet size, competition coeffs = preferences). (3) Numerical and economic rigor: they don't hand-wave the ODE solve — they name RKF45 with Cash-Karp coefficients and explain adaptive step control; the economic side uses proper present-worth/discounting with effective interest rates and a real Leaf-vs-Civic comparison that yields a concrete, decision-relevant answer (BEVs need subsidy today; competitive at $5/gal gas). Judges also reward the honest, prominent discussion of the model's biggest weakness (no calibration data for BEVs) and the well-organized scenario framework that turns one model into a policy sensitivity study. The executive summary leads with quantified headline results (53% CO2 cut, 70% oil cut, millions of jobs), which is exactly what judges skim for.
section_highlights: The Executive Summary is a model of MCM summary-writing: it opens with the policy thesis, states the core method (adapted Lotka-Volterra), and front-loads quantified headline results (53% CO2 reduction, 70% oil reduction over 20 years, several million jobs) before diving into scenarios — judges can grade the paper from this page alone. Section 2.1 cleanly presents the three governing ODEs (Eqs. 1-3) with a full variable/parameter table (Tables 0.1, 2.1, 2.2) giving every value, unit, and data source, so the model is fully reproducible. Section 2.1.1 demonstrates numerical literacy by naming RKF45/Cash-Karp and explaining adaptive step control rather than treating the solver as a black box. The Results section is organized as a disciplined scenario sweep (base + 4 scenarios), each with a semi-log figure and a single comparable metric (the G=E equivalence year), making the policy sensitivity instantly legible. Section 3.3/Table 3.2 is notably honest: it reports that EV adoption INCREASES some pollutants (PM10 +940%, SOx +860%, NOx +94%) by relocating emissions to coal plants — a counterintuitive, credibility-building result rather than a one-sided pro-EV pitch. The Discussion (Sec. 4) and Conclusions candidly flag the lack of calibration data as the primary weakness and lay out concrete further-study steps (couple economic+growth models, calibrate as data arrives, add stochasticity).
pitfalls_or_limitations: No model calibration or validation: parameters (growth rates, competition coefficients) were assigned by judgment, not fit to data, because almost no BEV market history existed in 2011 — the team admits this is the primary weakness

Sensitivity analysis was only done on the economic model (Table 3.1); the paper explicitly states the ODE/population model's sensitivity to its input parameters was NOT completed 'due to time constraints' — a real gap a top paper should have closed

Implausible/uninterpreted parameter values: intrinsic growth rates r = 1.52 /year (i.e., ~152%/yr) are extremely high for a vehicle fleet and are never reconciled with the stated 1% carrying-capacity growth; identical r and K=300M for all three classes undercut the differentiation the competition coefficients are meant to provide

Competition coefficients are 'lumped, deterministic' catch-alls for consumer preference, economics, policy, and technology simultaneously — so the model can reproduce almost any trajectory by tuning them, which limits predictive (vs. illustrative) value

Scenarios are defined by ad hoc, hand-chosen coefficient changes (e.g., 'increase alpha_E to 1.3, decrease beta_H to 0.6') with no systematic link between a stated dollar oil price and the resulting coefficient — the mapping from economics to parameters is asserted, not derived

Internal inconsistencies in the extracted tables/figures (e.g., equivalence year quoted as 2035 base then 2027/2018 best case in different places; figure data tables show order-of-magnitude axis labels that don't cleanly match the prose), and a notation collision where the variable table lists G(t) twice (for both ICE and PHEV)

Emission accounting assumes a static present-day US generation mix and ignores spatial distribution of pollution (acknowledged), so the dramatic PM/SOx increases are an upper bound that scrubbers and grid decarbonization would soften

The economic model and the population model are never actually coupled — fuel prices feed the present-worth analysis and the competition coefficients independently, so the two halves are correlated by narrative, not by shared equations (the team lists coupling them as future work)
reusable_patterns: Cross-domain analogy as the core model: when a prompt is about competition/adoption/substitution among a few options, a multi-species Lotka-Volterra (or Bass diffusion / replicator dynamics) system is a powerful, judge-pleasing engine — define the 'shared resource' and justify each term

Two-stage architecture: build a dynamics model that outputs the one quantity that drives everything (here market share over time), then multiply by per-unit factors from authoritative sources (GREET, EPA, EIA) to generate ALL the disparate impact metrics the problem asks for — this unifies an open-ended interdisciplinary prompt under one engine

Encode the problem's qualitative levers (price, policy, investment, technology) explicitly as named model parameters or initial conditions, then run a structured scenario sweep that perturbs them — converts a vague 'discuss impacts' prompt into a quantitative sensitivity study
Define a single scalar comparison metric across scenarios (the 'equivalence year' when two populations cross) so a reader can rank five complex simulations at a glance

Pair the dynamic model with an independent, concrete micro-level economic analysis (one Leaf vs one Civic via present-worth discounting) to ground abstract predictions in a tangible, decision-relevant number
Lead the executive summary with quantified headline outcomes and the method name in the first two sentences; reserve detail for the body
Report counterintuitive/unfavorable results honestly (EV adoption raising PM and SOx) — it builds credibility far more than a one-sided advocacy paper
Name and justify the numerical method (RKF45/Cash-Karp adaptive stepping) and cite the data source for every initial condition and parameter in a single consolidated table for reproducibility
Close with an explicit, actionable 'further study' list (couple the sub-models, calibrate when data arrives, add stochasticity) that pre-empts the reviewer's objections by acknowledging them
